metadata.career_relevance: Fast/slow pointer (Floyd's tortoise and hare) is the most elegant linked list pattern. 'Find the middle' and 'detect cycle' are asked in virtually every LinkedIn/Flipkart/Amazon linked list round. One pattern, many applications.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha: detect infinite loop in order processing pipeline
Order links: order A → order B → order C → order B (cycle!)
Processor will loop forever unless we detect the cycle

Naive: HashSet of visited nodes — O(n) time, O(n) space
Floyds: fast+slow pointers — O(n) time, O(1) space
Fast moves 2 steps, slow moves 1 step
If cycle exists: fast catches slow (like circular track)
If no cycle: fast reaches null
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Fast/slow pointer solutions: middle node, nth from end, cycle detection, intersection.

visual_aid.description: Two diagrams. Left: Find middle of 1→2→3→4→5. Slow/fast both start at 1. Iteration 1: slow=2,fast=3. Iteration 2: slow=3,fast=5. fast.next=null → stop. Middle=slow=3. Right: Cycle detection on 1→2→3→4→5→3(cycle). Iteration 1: slow=2,fast=3. Iter 2: slow=3,fast=5. Iter 3: slow=4,fast=4. slow==fast: CYCLE!
visual_aid.alt_text: Fast/slow pointer for middle detection and cycle detection

worked_example.scenario: Palindrome Linked List — check in O(n) O(1) space.
worked_example.problem_statement: Return true if linked list is palindrome. [1,2,2,1]→true, [1,2]→false.

step_by_step_solution.details: (1) Find middle with slow/fast. (2) Reverse second half in-place. (3) Compare first and second halves. (4) Optionally restore list.

things_to_notice: Find middle + reverse: O(n) time O(1) space — no extra array needed
Even length: slow stops at second middle — still works
This pattern (find middle, reverse half, compare) solves palindrome in optimal space
Restore: after comparison, reverse second half back — good practice in interviews

industry_examples.use_case: Detect circular reference in payment gateway routing (cycle = infinite retry loop). Floyd's O(1) space vs HashSet O(n).

applications.interview_relevance: 'Find middle', 'detect cycle', 'find cycle start', 'palindrome LL' — all fast/slow. Master one pattern, solve four problems.
applications.real_world_workflow: Fast/slow decision: (1) need middle? fast/slow. (2) detect cycle? fast/slow + meet check. (3) find cycle start? after meeting, reset slow to head, move both at speed 1. (4) palindrome? middle + reverse + compare.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Knowing how to detect a cycle but not how to find the cycle ENTRY point (where the cycle starts).
common_gaps.remediation: After fast and slow meet: reset slow to head. Move BOTH at speed 1. They meet at the cycle entry. Proof: distance from head to entry = distance from meet point to entry (mod cycle length).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student can detect cycle but returns meeting point instead of cycle start

key_takeaways: Fast=2 steps, slow=1 step → fast catches slow in O(n) if cycle
Find middle: stop when fast==null or fast.next==null
Cycle detection: fast==slow after start = cycle
Cycle start: after meeting, reset slow to head, both at speed 1, meet at start
Palindrome: find middle + reverse second half + compare
Nth from end: fast N steps ahead, both advance until fast==null

self_check_questions.question: Why does resetting slow to head and advancing both at speed 1 find the cycle entry?
self_check_questions.answer: Let d = distance from head to cycle entry, c = cycle length, k = distance from cycle entry to meeting point. When slow enters cycle at step d, fast has lapped it and is at position (2d - d) mod c = d mod c within the cycle. The meeting occurs when fast catches slow: after (c-k) more steps from the entry. The key property: d ≡ c - k (mod c), which means d and c-k are equivalent mod c. So resetting slow to head and advancing both at speed 1: slow travels d steps to reach the entry, fast travels d steps from the meeting point (which is c-k steps before the entry in the cycle direction) — they arrive at the entry simultaneously.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Linked List Reversal — full reverse, partial reverse (reverse between positions), reverse in k-groups.
